summary:
The tuberculin skin test may yield false-positive results in individuals who have been previously vaccinated with BCG due to cross-reaction between BCG-derived antigens and those found in Mycobacterium tuberculosis. This leads to significant misinterpretation, especially in settings where large portions of the population has received BCG vaccinations, making such tests unreliable for diagnosing active TB disease without further confirmation. 

Final Answer: $\boxed{\text{A}}$

The tuberculin skin test often produces false-positive outcomes in individuals with prior BCG vaccination because of cross-reactive immunity triggered by similar bacterial proteins. These false readings are particularly problematic in densely immunized populations, compromising accurate diagnosis when interpreting test results.  

$\boxed{\text{A}}$

Final summary only:

BCG vaccination increases risk of false-positive tuberculin test results through antigenic cross-reactivity among mycobacterial components. High prevalence of pre-existing BCG-immune cells leads to inaccurate interpretations despite lack of actual TB infection — most commonly seen in fully immunized populations.
